results.title: MEMT R1 - Reviews | Headphone Reviews and Discussion - Head-Fi.org
results.content:
Highs: Treble is polite, not to say they don't have good detail in the highs and even some sparkle but it always is well behaved even in the most demanding of songs.   
   
 Soundstage: Is wide and deep has good imaging and everything together just feels natural in its presentation.   
   
 Conclusion:  
 This is a great looking, well made IEM with a mature/ wide V-shaped signature many will find better than others in this price range. I think it stands out from the others with a sound I like and I look forward to more from this company. . [...] Technicalities Soundstage Above average, but don’t expect a gothic cathedral space effect.  
   
 Imaging Quite average, it gets messier on complex tracks due to lacking separation/layering skils (see below).  
   
 Details All those trebles do pull out a lot of details from the high voice instruments, which is good although limited by scarce layering refinement. Certainly way above average for this price bracket.  
   
 Instrument separation Separation and moreover layering is quite lacking. On complex tracks this becomes, simply put, a mess. Together with bright preponderance (which may be less of a problem for some) this is evidently the worst part of the product (and it will be for everyone I’m afraid). [...] Hooga

#### Hooga

##### 100+ Head-Fier

 

 3.50 star(s)

MEMT R1 : an attempt at something different

Pros: Nice, clean & dynamic lows.  
 Engaging trebles.  
 Good details.  
 Nice looks and great comfort

Cons: Bright-unbalanced tuning (some may like it).  
 Thin vocals.  
 Lacking in separation / layering.  
 Tendent to sibilance (good luck with tip rolling).

results.title: Signs You Might Be Struggling with Your Mental Health – and What to Do Next | NAMI Metro Suburban
results.content:
|  |

| Call NAMI Metro Suburban for Support: 708-524-2582 |

, some of the most common early warning signs of mental health issues include changes in sleep or appetite, excessive worrying, low energy, and mood shifts. These are things most people experience now and then. But when they begin to interfere with your daily life (your ability to function, focus, or connect with others) it may be time to take a closer look.

And here’s something important: everyone has mental health, and we all experience anxiety or sadness from time to time. That doesn’t mean you’re broken. It just means you’re human. But if those feelings start to weigh you down, you don’t have to wait for them to get worse to reach out.

## Signs You Might Be Struggling [...] Talk to Someone You Trust

You don’t have to go through this alone. Opening up to a friend, family member, or counselor can lighten the emotional load and remind you that support is closer than it feels.

Try Not to Isolate

It’s tempting to shut down when you’re overwhelmed, but small moments of connection (sending a text, joining a support group, or just being around others) can break the spiral of loneliness and help you reconnect with hope.

## How NAMI Metro Suburban Can Help

If you’re feeling unsure of your next step, NAMI Metro Suburban offers judgment-free, people-first support right in your community. We can meet you right where you are. [...] ## What You Can Do Today

You don’t need a full plan to start feeling better. Sometimes, it’s the smallest changes that bring the biggest relief. Here are a few things you can do right now:

Start Journaling or Tracking Your Emotions

Writing things down helps make sense of what you’re feeling, and research backs this up. A meta-analysis published in PMC found that journaling led to a 9% decrease in anxiety and a 2% decrease in depression scores across participants. Even five minutes a day can offer much-needed relief.

Make Sleep a Priority

results.title: 6 Signs you Should be Concerned About Your Mental Health
results.content:
### 2. Irritability or being more emotional than usual.

Experiencing irritation, anger, feeling snappy and easily frustrated, or mood swings that fly from one extreme to the other could be a sign that your mental health is out of whack. Depression and anxiety can make it harder to self-regulate your thoughts and feelings, which is why you may be more reactive or sensitive than usual.

### 3. Loss of joy. [...] ### 5. Worsening physical symptoms.

Depression and anxiety can bring on physical side effects, including sweating, rapid heart rate, dizziness, gastrointestinal symptoms, and headache. If physical symptoms come on suddenly with no other medical cause, it could be a sign that your mental health is declining.

### 6. Low energy.

Feelings of fatigue and lethargy are also common in people who are struggling with their mental health. Feeling mentally or physically sluggish can make it harder to concentrate, follow conversations, or think quickly. If you have low energy to the point where it’s hard to find the motivation to get out of bed, consider talking to your doctor.

## How to improve your mental health at home. [...] Aiming for 7-8 hours of sleep per night with no screen time for 30 minutes before bed
 Eating healthy, balanced meals that consist of whole foods
 Exercising for 30 minutes a day, at least five days per week
 Hydrating well with water, eliminating excessive caffeine
 Practicing mindfulness and meditation
 Talking to someone you trust and consider safely expanding your social bubble
 Avoiding excessive alcohol consumption (Women should drink fewer than 8 alcoholic beverages per week. Men should drink fewer than 14)

## When to see a doctor.

If healthy habits still don’t improve your mental health, don’t be ashamed to seek help from a professional. Talk to your primary care provider about what you’re going through and be prepared to answer questions related to your:
